message:
feat(codec): CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED + check_compat helper, wire into client handshake (ft-kuxho.B.1)

Step 1 of the rolling-upgrade window rollout per
docs/proposals/ft-kuxho-B-codec-version-min-supported-window.md.

Adds:
  - pub const CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED: usize = 46
    (bootstrap value == CODEC_VERSION; advancing it is a breaking
    change gated by docs/codec-versions.md + ft-8smkj CI guard)
  - pub enum CompatDecision::Compatible { agreed: usize }
  - pub struct CompatError { local, local_min, remote, remote_min }
    with thiserror Display surfacing both triples + docs/codec-atomic-redeploy.md link
  - pub fn check_compat(local, local_min, remote, remote_min)
    -> Result<CompatDecision, CompatError>
    Compatible iff [max(local_min, remote_min), min(local, remote)]
    is non-empty; agreed = min(local, remote) so the lower-versioned
    peer dictates the dialect and the higher peer's additive PDU
    extensions stay in the consumed-but-ignored frame tail
    (ft-e1emx tail-padding property).

Wires into the client handshake in frankenterm/client/src/client.rs:
the strict-equality `info.codec_vers == CODEC_VERSION` gate is
replaced with codec::check_compat(...). Until ft-kuxho.B.3 lands,
GetCodecVersionResponse does not carry a remote min_supported, so
remote_min is conservatively passed as remote (server treated as
supporting only its own version). With the bootstrap value of
CODEC_VERSION_MIN_SUPPORTED == CODEC_VERSION the behavior is
strict-equality-equivalent today; once both sides advertise minima,
the window opens up. A log::warn! fires when agreed != CODEC_VERSION
so operators see the compat-window negotiation in production logs.

5 unit tests (codec::test::check_compat_*):
  - bootstrap_min_equals_version (invariant)
  - same_version_both_sides (a)
  - local_newer_inside_remote_window (b, both directions)
  - local_below_remote_minimum_is_incompatible (c, plus error msg
    asserts both triples and the runbook link appear)
  - agrees_on_lower_version_within_window (the wide-window case;
    pins the "agreed = min(local, remote)" semantic)

cargo test -p codec check_compat: 5/5 pass.
cargo check -p wezterm-client: clean.

Closes ft-kuxho.1.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-6mxfs","title":"[mor:security-engineering] ws_dir = root.join('.ft','patterns') is not canonicalized before discovery","description":"# Observation

In `load_packs_from_config`
(crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:949):

    let ws_dir = root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns");
    let discovered = discover_packs_from_dir(&ws_dir)?;

ft-fbee5 (just closed) fixed the case where a symlink INSIDE
`.ft/patterns/` escapes the sandbox. But the parent path is itself
just a join, no canonicalize. A hostile cloned repo can ship:

    .ft/patterns -> /etc

(a symlink at the directory level.) `ws_dir` canonicalizes to
`/etc`, `discover_packs_from_dir(/etc)` iterates there, and the
per-file sandbox check passes because it canonicalizes with root =
`/etc`. The symlink escape is now one level up.

Similar attack surface for `config.resolved_user_packs_dir()` if
that ever returns a joined path the caller doesn't canonicalize.

# Question

Should `load_packs_from_config` canonicalize `ws_dir` and verify the
result resolves *inside* `root_path.canonicalize()` before calling
`discover_packs_from_dir`? That closes the parent-level symlink
vector without needing to change `discover_packs_from_dir`'s
contract.

Stretch: is there a case for centralizing this as a
`sandbox_resolve_dir(dir, root)` helper and using it everywhere the
pack/workflow/policy loaders join a workspace-relative path? The
pattern is probably not unique to pack loading.

# Trust boundary

The repo is the attacker; the user's home directory is the victim.
Everything a hostile repo can make `read_to_string` see is a
potential data-exfil primitive. Treat repo-relative path
construction as crossing a trust boundary, and canonicalize at the
boundary crossing, not at the leaf.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:35:09.320199Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.174562Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:51:59.305207Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 0239fbd6 — added sandbox_resolve_dir helper, routed load_packs_from_config's ws_dir through it with 4 regression tests (missing, inside-root, toplevel-symlink-escape, end-to-end).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mor","sandboxing","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-6piwt","title":"[P0 BLOCKER] frankenterm-core lib has 61 compile errors under --no-default-features — blocks all --no-default-features test/bench runs","description":"## Symptom
`cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features` fails with 61 errors. Panes 3 and 4 in the swarm both hit this while trying to verify their respective commits.

## Representative error
```
error[E0599]: no method named `record_event_with_cx` found for this struct
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs:6157
  help: there is a method `record_event` with a similar name

error[E0599]: no method named `get_text_with_cx` found for struct `std::sync::Arc<(dyn WeztermInterface + 'static)>`
  --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:3272:43
  note: `wezterm::PaneTextSource` defines an item `get_text_with_cx`, perhaps you need to implement it
```

## Root cause hypothesis
Asupersync migration landed `_with_cx` **callers** in some files without landing `_with_cx` **implementations** on the corresponding traits/structs, specifically under the default feature set (where the legacy impls are gated off but the new impls aren't gated on).

## Scope
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/storage.rs (record_event_with_cx)
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs (get_text_with_cx + PaneTextSource trait alignment)
- 61 errors total; likely same pattern elsewhere

## Fix direction
1. Run `cargo check -p frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features 2>&1 | grep 'error\[\|error:' > /tmp/errors.txt` to enumerate all 61.
2. Group by method name; for each missing `*_with_cx` method:
   - If the non-cx variant exists, add a default impl or route `with_cx` through it (respects cancel semantics).
   - If neither exists, revert the caller to the non-cx variant with a TODO referencing ft-xbnl0.2 epic.
3. Verify with `cargo check` clean, then `cargo test --lib --no-default-features`.

## Priority
P0 — blocks swarm from running any tests under --no-default-features, which is the recommended lean test surface.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:29:56.725238Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680762Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:50:22.680537Z","close_reason":"Added no-feature cx compatibility shims and ungated fallback *_with_cx adapters so frankenterm-core --lib --no-default-features compiles again","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-761tz","title":"[HIGH] disk_pressure NaN sample silently reports Green tier instead of failing closed","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller passes `DiskSample { usage_fraction: NaN, .. }` to `DiskPressureMonitor::update_with_sample`. Easy trigger: `available/total` when `total == 0` (e.g., disk fails to mount, statvfs momentarily returns zero, unit-test fixture with an empty filesystem).
2. `EwmaEstimator::update` calls `sample.clamp(0.0, 1.0)`. **`NaN.clamp(0.0, 1.0)` returns NaN** — this is documented Rust behaviour but caller-hostile. The clamp does not sanitise the NaN.
3. `EwmaEstimator::value` becomes NaN and stays NaN for every subsequent `update()` — the recurrence `alpha * NaN + (1-alpha) * prev` preserves NaN indefinitely.
4. `PidController::update` receives `pid_error = NaN - target`. Its internal integral + derivative go NaN and the output is NaN. Permanent until `reset()`.
5. `classify_tier(NaN, thresholds)` compares `NaN >= thresholds.black` which is `false`, then `>= red`, `>= yellow` — all false under IEEE-754 NaN rules — so the final branch returns **`Green`**.
6. Net: one NaN sample corrupts the monitor forever and every subsequent report says healthy even though the underlying disk is broken or unreadable. Operators trusting the Green tier take no action.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer, not a crash or a visible error.
- Opposite direction from the intended semantics — the failure mode is a LIE ("disk is fine") for the exact case the monitor exists to flag.
- One bad sample is sticky: the monitor cannot self-recover.

## Fix sketch

1. `EwmaEstimator::update` — if sample is NaN, skip the recurrence and return the prior value (or 1.0 if uninitialised). Do not poison internal state.
2. `PidController::update` — if error is NaN, skip state update and return 0.0 (or last output). Same no-poison rule.
3. `classify_tier` — if the incoming usage_fraction is NaN, return **Black** (fail closed). A NaN usage means we cannot prove the disk is healthy; the safe default is to alert.
4. Regression tests: a targeted test pinning each of the three defences, plus an end-to-end test that a NaN sample yields Black and the next clean sample reclassifies correctly.

## Scope

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/disk_pressure.rs` only. No cross-crate API changes.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:39:31.506608Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:14.150284Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:14.149967Z","close_reason":"Landed in c5a02edb (fix(disk_pressure): fail closed on NaN in Ewma, PID, and classifier) + regression tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-7ojbj","title":"rate_limit_tracker: parse_retry_after can produce Duration that panics Instant + cooldown","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/rate_limit_tracker.rs:441-473` `parse_retry_after` has no upper bound on the parsed duration.

Plain-number path at :452-454:
```rust
if let Ok(secs) = text.parse::<u64>() {
    return Some(Duration::from_secs(secs));
}
```

'N unit' path at :458-469 has a `checked_mul` guard, but the plain number path returns `Duration::from_secs(u64::MAX)` untouched (u64::MAX secs ≈ 584 billion years).

The returned Duration flows into `PaneRateLimitState::record_event` at :164:
```rust
let expiry = event.detected_at + event.cooldown;
```

`Instant + Duration` panics when the sum overflows the platform's underlying representation. On macOS/Linux Instant is backed by clock_gettime timespec/uptime — adding ~584B years to a fresh Instant will overflow.

# Reachability

`retry_after_text` reaches record_event from pattern detection output. `trigger_retry_after` (workflows/builtin_workflows.rs) extracts the string from agent-emitted rate-limit messages. A malformed provider response or adversarial agent output ('Retry-After: 18446744073709551615') would panic the tracker on record.

# Existing tests

`parse_retry_after_overflow_returns_none` at :515-518 covers the 'N unit' checked_mul path with 'u64::MAX hours', but NOT the plain-number path with 'u64::MAX'. That path currently returns `Some` unclamped.

# Fix

Clamp parse_retry_after to a sane max (24h ceiling — legitimate provider Retry-After values never exceed 1h in practice, and any longer wait is a signal to bail rather than block the tracker). Apply the cap uniformly to plain-number and 'N unit' paths. Also clamp DEFAULT_COOLDOWN_SECS fallback if it ever grows.

Add regression test: parse_retry_after_plain_u64_max_is_clamped asserts the plain-number path returns a Duration ≤ the cap, not None and not u64::MAX.

# Severity

MEDIUM — requires adversarial/malformed provider input to trigger. Tracker is behind rate_limit_tracker.lock() in workflows/builtin_workflows.rs so the panic takes down the workflow task but is scoped to one pane's rate-limit processing. Fix is trivial: one `.min(MAX_COOLDOWN)` call plus one test.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T09:40:08.211722Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:10.213965Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T09:50:10.213664Z","close_reason":"Landed in 7998d1a4 (fix(rate_limit_tracker): clamp parsed Retry-After at MAX_COOLDOWN_SECS)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-7s0vw","title":"event_id::detect_clock_anomaly overflows on prev + future_skew_threshold","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/event_id.rs:205\` computes
\`prev + future_skew_threshold\` without overflow protection. In release
builds u64 wraps silently; in debug builds the unchecked add panics.
Either way, a pathological or adversarial \`prev\` value breaks the
future-skew branch.

## Concrete failure path

1. Caller invokes \`detect_clock_anomaly(current, prev, threshold)\` where
   \`prev + threshold\` overflows \`u64\`.
2. Easy reach: replay/import flows feeding \`recorded_at_ms = u64::MAX\` from
   a corrupt fixture, fuzzer, or attacker-controlled input. Also reachable
   via legitimate-but-distant clock sources (UNIX epoch micros around year
   586,524 if anyone reaches that, or any monotonic clock that has been
   bumped by a process bug into the high u64 range).
3. **Release build:** \`prev + threshold\` wraps to a tiny value. The
   condition \`current > tiny_value\` is now true for almost any \`current\`,
   so the function returns \`is_anomaly: true\` for every subsequent valid
   timestamp — flooding telemetry with false-positive "future skew"
   reports and potentially triggering downstream remediation that should
   not have fired.
4. **Debug build:** unchecked add panics (\`attempt to add with overflow\`),
   killing whatever thread was processing the recorder pipeline.
5. \`ClockAnomalyTracker::observe\` always updates the baseline
   ("Updates the baseline regardless") — so a single malicious or
   transiently-high \`current\` value sticks around and continues to
   poison overflow checks against subsequent observations.

## Severity rationale

- Silent wrong answer in release; thread-killing panic in debug.

   silence rather than toward false positives.

   prev=u64::MAX → no anomaly, no panic; (b) overflow with threshold=
   u64::MAX and small prev → no anomaly, no panic; (c) the existing
   future-skew path still fires when math is in range.

## Scope

three whitelists of tools/resources/templates expected to be db-gated:

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [
    \"wa.accounts\",
    \"wa.events\",
    \"wa.reserve\",
    \"wa.release\",
    \"wa.search\",
    \"wa.send\",
    \"wa.workflow_run\",
] {
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

The assertion only checks absence from \`no_db_tools\`. If a tool is
renamed (e.g., \`wa.accounts\` → \`wa.list_accounts\`) or removed entirely,
the whitelist entry silently stays true (\`!no_db_tools.contains(\"wa.accounts\")\`
is trivially true when \`wa.accounts\` no longer exists in either manifest).

The same drift pattern applies to the resource whitelist
(lines ~285, \`wa://accounts\`, \`wa://events\`, \`wa://reservations\`) and
the template whitelist (lines ~295, \`wa://accounts/{service}\` etc.).

Impact: the no-db subset guard gives false confidence. A tool could
accidentally become always-on (or be removed from db-gated registration)
without the test catching it.

Fix: for each whitelist entry, assert \`full_tools.contains(entry)\`
BEFORE checking \`!no_db_tools.contains(entry)\`. That way, if the
whitelisted tool disappears from the db-backed manifest, the test
fails and forces whoever made the change to update the whitelist
deliberately.

\`\`\`rust
for expected_absent_tool in [...] {
    assert!(
        full_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"whitelist drift: {expected_absent_tool} no longer exists in db-backed manifest — update the test\"
    );
    assert!(
        !no_db_tools.contains(expected_absent_tool),
        \"db-gated tool unexpectedly advertised without db_path: {expected_absent_tool}\"
    );
}
\`\`\`

Severity rationale: medium — test-quality issue that masks regressions
in MCP tool surface categorization. Not a correctness bug in production
code.

 {"id":"ft-g3z31","title":"[review][LOW] ft-ojgmo lacks regression test for pre-cancel fast-path in EventSubscriber::recv_cx","description":"fa1fbffe added two cancel-detection paths to EventSubscriber::recv_cx at crates/frankenterm-core/src/events.rs:789-817:

1. Pre-cancel fast-path at :793:
       if cx.is_cancel_requested() { return Err(RecvError::Cancelled); }
2. Mid-flight cancel watcher at :798-806 (select race with 50ms polling).

The new regression test `event_subscriber_recv_cx_surfaces_mid_flight_cancel` at :1573-1601 exercises the MID-FLIGHT path only — cancel is signalled 100ms AFTER recv_cx is entered. The PRE-CANCEL fast-path (cx already cancelled when recv_cx is called) is untested at the EventSubscriber layer.

Evidence: search for calls to EventSubscriber::recv_cx with a pre-cancelled cx in the test suite — none exist. The runtime_compat.rs test at :6189 tests pre-cancel on the lower-level broadcast_recv_with_cx primitive, but not the EventSubscriber wrapper that is the public API.

Why it matters: the pre-cancel shortcut is one line, but it was the difference between a hot loop of polling wakeups and immediate return. A refactor that moves the cancel check inside the select! block (losing the shortcut) would regress the behavior silently — the mid-flight test would still pass because the 50ms poll would eventually fire.

Fix: add a sibling test:

    #[test]
    fn event_subscriber_recv_cx_surfaces_pre_cancel_immediately() {
        run_async_test(async {
            let cx = crate::cx::Cx::for_testing();
            cx.cancel_with(crate::outcome::CancelKind::User, Some("pre-cancel"));
            let bus = EventBus::new(8);
            let mut sub = bus.subscribe();
            let started = Instant::now();
            let result = sub.recv_cx(&cx).await;
            assert!(matches!(result, Err(RecvError::Cancelled)));
            assert!(started.elapsed() < Duration::from_millis(5),
                "pre-cancel must return immediately, took {:?}", started.elapsed());
        });
    }

A <5ms assertion distinguishes the shortcut path from the 50ms poll path.

Severity: LOW — not a correctness bug, just a coverage gap. Discovered during reviewer audit round 11, 2026-04-23 while verifying fa1fbffe.

 {"id":"ft-hyrav","title":"events::NotificationCooldown::check: insertion_order not refreshed on cooldown-expired send, LRU evicts actively-used keys","description":"crates/frankenterm-core/src/events.rs:1015-1064 NotificationCooldown::check has a subtle LRU-staleness bug: when a key's cooldown has EXPIRED and we emit Send (line 1036-1043), we update entry.last_notified to now and reset entry.suppressed_since_notify, but we do NOT move the key to the back of self.insertion_order. The key stays at its ORIGINAL insertion position. Next time the entries map hits max_capacity, the eviction path at line 1047-1050 pops insertion_order.pop_front() — which picks a key by ORIGINAL INSERTION ORDER, not by last-use. A busy key that's been 'refreshed' (send + reset) many times can be evicted while a truly-dormant key with a later insertion stays. Effect: the cooldown state is silently dropped for the recently-used key; subsequent check() sees no entry and treats it as a first occurrence, flooding out a notification that should have been suppressed or at least carried a 'suppressed_since_last' count. Same pattern already fixed in sibling EventDeduplicator at events.rs:867-870 (refresh insertion_order on window-expired reset). Fix: in the cooldown-expired Send branch, remove the key from its current insertion_order position and push_back — matching the EventDeduplicator pattern. Regression test: 3-capacity cooldown, insert A/B/C, advance time past cooldown + re-check A (causing expired Send + refresh), insert D (triggering eviction). Pre-fix A is evicted; post-fix B is evicted (truly oldest-by-use).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:33:24.607054Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:34:50.222010Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:34:50.221646Z","close_reason":"Landed in 36c6f7d3 (fix(events): refresh NotificationCooldown insertion_order on cooldown-expired send).  {"id":"ft-n3mim","title":"[mor:ergonomics] Error::Runtime(String) is the '3am diagnostic dead-end' variant","description":"# Observation

Memory and grep confirm the frankenterm-core Error enum has no
\`Generic\` variant — the convention is \`Runtime(String)\` for
ad-hoc failures. Every time a caller needed a fresh error shape and
didn't want to define a typed variant, they reached for
\`Error::Runtime\`.

At 3am during an incident, the operator sees:

    Error: runtime: mux pool write failed: io error: Broken pipe (os error 32)

This is a String wrapped in an enum variant. You can't match on it,
you can't branch on it programmatically, you can't build a
dashboard row off it. The information that would make the incident
tractable — is this a transient network blip, a crashed mux, a
kernel panic, a rate limit, a disk-full? — is buried in a
string the operator has to grep for.

# Question

For each subsystem that currently stuffs into \`Error::Runtime(...)\`,
is there a typed variant that would make triage faster? Specific
candidates worth auditing:

- \`runtime_compat\` cancel / timeout wrappers — currently stringify
  the underlying failure into Runtime
- \`mux_pool\` write/read failures that aren't already covered by
  \`WeztermError\`
- \`asupersync\` lifecycle errors the caller needs to distinguish
  from transient I/O

A principle: every error the operator would act on differently
deserves a typed variant. Everything else can stay generic.

# 3am impact

This is the class of issue that doesn't show up in normal workflow.
It only bites when production is on fire. The cost is invisible
until the cost is enormous. The fix is incremental: every Runtime
creation site is a candidate for typing, and typing them one-at-a-
time pays immediately in the next incident that touches that
subsystem.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:36:27.030250Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:49.450481Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:57.270015Z","close_reason":"implemented","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["3am","ergonomics","error-types","mor"],"comments":[{"id":3600,"issue_id":"ft-n3mim","author":"jemanuel","text":"Commit 79702a50 introduces typed top-level runtime/pane/watchdog error variants plus source enums in crates/frankenterm-core/src/error.rs, deprecates Error::Runtime as a last-resort catch-all, and migrates clean operator-facing call-sites in crates/frankenterm-core/src/export.rs, crates/frankenterm-core/src/search_explain.rs, and crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs away from stringly Runtime(...). Added regression coverage for typed pane/watchdog/runtime classification and mock WezTerm error context. Local verification: rustfmt + git diff --check passed on the touched files. Requested rch lane (structured_error_ test prefix) is still in full-workspace sync on worker vmi1227854 with no compile/test result line yet. Note: crates/frankenterm-core/src/runtime.rs still has unrelated live edits in this checkout, so I intentionally did not widen this bead into that file.","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:52:57Z"}]}
 {"id":"ft-n4fdx","title":"bayesian_ledger::BayesianLedger::update accepts non-finite Evidence which permanently poisons log_posterior with NaN","description":"Evidence variants OutputRate(f64), Entropy(f64), TimeSinceOutput(f64), ScrollbackGrowth(f64) are passed unchecked into gaussian_log_likelihoods and time_since_output_log_likelihoods.  {"id":"ft-njlk0","title":"docs: add SECURITY.md with fix history","description":"The repo has no SECURITY.md (neither at root nor under `docs/`).
Downstream users and contributors currently have no canonical
place to find:

1. the project's security posture and trust boundaries,
2. how to report a suspected vulnerability privately,
3. the audit trail of shipped security fixes.

Pane 5's 2026-04-20 → 2026-04-21 security audit shipped 7 fixes
across 8 audit passes. Beads are the current record; SECURITY.md
would give operators and auditors a single readable entry point.

## Proposed SECURITY.md scope

### § Reporting vulnerabilities
- Preferred channel (placeholder: maintainer email / GitHub private
  advisory).
- What to include in a report (repro, affected version, workspace
  config shape).
- Expected response SLO (e.g. 7d acknowledgement, 30d fix
  timeline for MEDIUM+).
- Explicit out-of-scope: DoS on unsupported platforms, findings
  that require pre-existing DB write access (captured in ft-kegvt
  / ft-ybtyg close-reasons).

### § Trust boundaries
- MCP stdio transport inherits OS uid/gid; no in-band auth.
- `.ft/` directory contents are treated as attacker-reachable
  (hostile repo clone is a real threat model).
- `ft watch` runs as the invoking user; no privilege separation.
- Pane output captured into storage is redacted on READ surfaces,
  NOT at ingest; DB file permissions are the containment boundary.

### § Fix history (session 2026-04-20 / 2026-04-21)

| Severity | Commit | Summary |
|----------|--------|---------|
| CRITICAL | 30988e53 | MCP `contract_file` / `mission_file` containment — blocks arbitrary R/W under watcher UID via hostile path args; `compute_workspace_scoped_path` with `..` reject + canonicalize + `starts_with(root_canon)`. |
| HIGH | 070117a0 | Pattern-engine ReDoS cap — `compile_rule_regex` via `RegexBuilder::new(raw).backtrack_limit(10M)` at all compile sites; blocks hostile `.ft/patterns/*.yaml` DoS. |
| MEDIUM | 969b9e02 | Restored-command CR/LF/ESC/C0 sanitizer in `launch_agent_{legacy,cx}`; blocks shell injection via tampered `mux_pane_state.command`. |
| MEDIUM | 598a7c13 | Session-checkpoint state_hash: replaced hardcoded `'restore'` literal with domain-separated SipHash-24 over `(session_id, pane_id_map, now_ms)`; docs realigned from misleading "BLAKE3" to accurate "SipHash-24 — not cryptographic integrity". |
| LOW | e790f481 | Diag bundle privacy scrubber — `gather_environment` routes `cwd` through `scrub_path_to_basename`; `DiagnosticOptions.include_full_cwd: false` by default; blocks `/Users/<name>/project-name` leak in shared bundles. |
| LOW | 0f38972b | `frankenterm-mux-server` SIGTERM/SIGINT handler — async-signal-safe `AtomicBool` flag polled by executor loop; ensures `wezterm_blob_leases::clear_storage()` runs on graceful shutdown instead of SIGKILL-skip. |
| LOW/MEDIUM | e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 | Size caps — `MAX_PACK_BYTES = 16 MiB` pre-parse stat check in `load_pack_from_file`; `MAX_SEND_TEXT_BYTES = 4 MiB` pre-dispatch in `WaSendTool::call`; blocks hostile-pack OOM and runaway `wa.send` payload. |

### § Regression guards
0aa93816 added victory-lap tests:
- symlink-escape rejection for MCP containment (relative + absolute variants, #[cfg(unix)]),
- ReDoS-budget headroom proof on a 100 KiB realistic input.

### § Non-normative: audit session findings classified as
defense-in-depth documentation only
- ft-yv02l (mock-code-finder catalog, closed benign)
- ft-2guux (reality-check: README cwd claim, Unix-only `ft restart`)

## Acceptance criteria
1. `/SECURITY.md` exists at repo root (the conventional GitHub
   location so it surfaces in the Security tab).
2. Sections match the four headings above.
3. Every commit hash in the Fix history table links to the
   correct git object (spot-check with `git show <hash>`).
4. Reporting channel is operator-decided — this bead should ask
   the maintainer before committing a specific contact.

## Out of scope
- Signing / provenance infrastructure (separate lane).
- Formal threat model diagram (scope creep for a docs bead).
- Translating fix-history entries into CVE identifiers (no CVE
  pipeline currently).

## Priority rationale
P2: no active exploit, no shipped user-visible gap — but the
SECURITY.md absence is a contributor-onboarding obstacle and
a GitHub "Security Policy" badge gap that every serious repo
should close.

- The seven fix commits listed above.
- 0aa93816 regression guards.

 {"id":"ft-nk6u9","title":"[MEDIUM] simd_scan ansi_byte_count misses OSC/DCS/PM/APC string bodies (terminates on first letter after ESC)","description":"# Defect

## Concrete failure path

`scan_newlines_and_ansi_*` (4 variants in `crates/frankenterm-core/src/simd_scan.rs`) treats any ASCII letter (0x40..=0x7E, excluding `[`) immediately after ESC as the terminator of the escape sequence. That rule is correct for CSI (`ESC [ … letter`) and for single-char C1 controls like `ESC M` (reverse index). It is **wrong** for the five C1 introducers that open a *string* sequence:

| Intro byte | Sequence | Continues until |
|------------|----------|-----------------|
| `]` (0x5D) | OSC (Operating System Command — window titles, clipboard, hyperlinks) | ST (`ESC \\`) or BEL (0x07) |
| `P` (0x50) | DCS (Device Control String — e.g., Sixel graphics, DECRQSS replies) | ST |
| `^` (0x5E) | PM (Privacy Message) | ST |
| `_` (0x5F) | APC (Application Program Command — e.g., Kitty graphics) | ST |
| `X` (0x58) | SOS (Start Of String) | ST |

All five land in the `0x40..=0x7E && != '['` branch and prematurely exit the escape. The string body + ST/BEL bytes are then counted as **plain text**, not ANSI.

## Reproducer (currently NO test covers this)

```rust
let data = b"\x1b]0;window title\x07after";
let scan = scan_newlines_and_ansi(data);
// Current:  ansi_byte_count = 2  (just ESC + ])
// Correct:  ansi_byte_count = 19 (ESC + ] + "0;window title" + BEL)
assert_eq!(scan.ansi_byte_count, 19);
```

Same bug in the `_with_state` variants — the cross-chunk state carries only `in_escape: bool` with no notion of "string mode," so the first chunk's body bytes leak into the "non-ANSI" count.

## Blast radius

- `ansi_density` (`OutputScanMetrics::ansi_density`) underreports on panes that emit OSC/DCS/PM/APC/SOS sequences — common in production: window-title updates from shell prompts, terminfo hyperlinks, Sixel graphics, Kitty image protocol, DCS replies to DECRQSS probes.
- `ScanMetricsSummary` is consumed by compression and backpressure decision paths; an underreport makes the system think a pane is producing plain text when it is actually producing high-ANSI output.
- Not a crash, not data loss — silent wrong answer in the metrics.

## Fix plan

1. Extend `OutputScanState` with a `StringPhase` — `InString` and `InStringSawEsc` so the stateful scanner can resume a partial `ESC \\` terminator across chunk boundaries.
2. After ESC is seen, if the next byte is one of \{0x50, 0x58, 0x5D, 0x5E, 0x5F\}, transition to `InString` instead of clearing `in_escape`.
3. While in `InString`: count every byte as ANSI; terminate on BEL (0x07) or ESC \\ (0x1b 0x5c).
4. Patch all four scan functions (memchr + scalar, stateful + non-stateful).
5. Add targeted regression tests: basic OSC, OSC + ST, OSC + BEL, DCS, PM, APC, chunk split through the body, chunk split on ESC-\\, and OSC containing embedded newlines (verify newline_count is unaffected).

Scope: single file (`simd_scan.rs`). No public-API break; `OutputScanState` gains a field but `Default` still returns the "no carry" state.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:07:00.334737Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:13:27.862859Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:13:27.862491Z","close_reason":"Landed in 99703277 (feat(simd_scan): add string-phase state machine for OSC/DCS/SOS/PM/APC byte counting)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
-{"id":"ft-nm5nc","title":"[MEDIUM] retire `not(feature = "asupersync-runtime")` legacy-tokio test/bench bodies (ft-f6vhg follow-up)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-26T02:36:49.642315Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-26T02:37:31.618490Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["architecture","refactor","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-nm5nc","depends_on_id":"ft-f6vhg","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-04-26T02:37:31.617807Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3642,"issue_id":"ft-nm5nc","author":"jemanuel","text":"# Why this exists

ft-f6vhg's premise was "asupersync-runtime is a no-op feature flag —
delete it." Verification under that bead found the feature gates a
substantial test surface:

  * ~50 test files use `#![cfg(feature = "asupersync-runtime")]` at the
    top of the file to opt into asupersync-specific Cx-aware APIs
    (sleep_with_cx, reserve(&cx), timeout_with_cx, etc.).
  * ~10 bench files do the same.
  * ~16 [[bench]] / [[test]] declarations use `required-features =
    ["asupersync-runtime"]` to mirror the file-level gate.
  * Several files (runtime_compat_tests.rs, robot_sdk_transport_integration.rs,
    proptest_runtime_compat.rs, replay_labruntime.rs, web.rs,
    proptest_runtime_compat.rs, mux_pool_scaling.rs, tailer.rs) carry
    PAIRED `#[cfg(feature = "asupersync-runtime")]` and
    `#[cfg(not(feature = "asupersync-runtime"))]` bodies. The
    `not(...)` body uses the legacy tokio-shape API (`reserve()`
    with no Cx, etc.) — it would break to compile against today's
    asupersync runtime if the feature were removed.

ft-xbnl0.2.5 made asupersync the sole async runtime, so the legacy
tokio-shape `not(...)` bodies are dead code in production. They are
kept around as "migration conformance" probes that compile only when
the feature is OFF (i.e., only under `--no-default-features`). That's
what's blocking the feature flag's deletion.

# What needs to happen

1. Audit each `#[cfg(not(feature = "asupersync-runtime"))]` body and
   decide:
   * If the body is genuinely tokio-shape and obsolete, DELETE it (keep
     the `#[cfg(feature = "asupersync-runtime")]` body, drop both
     gates, body becomes unconditional).
   * If the body is targeting a legacy API surface that's still alive
     elsewhere, file a per-site follow-up.
2. Once all `not(...)` paired bodies are cleaned, the file-level
   `#![cfg(feature = "asupersync-runtime")]` gates can be deleted
   (those test/bench files become unconditional).
3. THEN the feature definition can be removed from Cargo.toml + every
   `required-features` reference + the default-features list.

This is mechanical-but-careful work spanning ~60 files. Not safe to
batch-edit; each `not(...)` site needs an eyeball pass.

# Scope guard

Don't try to rename `runtime_compat` to `runtime_async` (ft-7iof6.1)
in the same pass — that's a different mechanical sweep with its own
deprecation cycle.

# Inputs

Cargo.toml comment at crates/frankenterm-core/Cargo.toml:677-687
(updated under ft-f6vhg) describes the test selector role and points
back at this bead. The 50 test files and 10 benches with file-level
gates are easily found via:

  rg '#!\[cfg\(.*"asupersync-runtime"' crates/frankenterm-core/{tests,benches}

The paired `not(...)` sites:

  rg 'cfg\(not\(feature = "asupersync-runtime"\)\)' crates/frankenterm-core","created_at":"2026-04-26T02:37:11Z"}]}
